# Veterinary telemedicine: Understanding its misconceptions and benefits

Dr. Crista Wallis, an exotics veterinarian and virtual care consultant based in Kansas, once decided to spend an entire month practicing veterinary medicine remotely from Florida.

While she consulted remotely with clients, her [trusted technician performed hands-on assessments](https://www.provet.cloud/blog/are-you-underutilizing-your-veterinary-technicians), ran diagnostics, and dispensed medications per her orders. They used a well-thought-out communication protocol and tools like a digital stethoscope to transmit accurate data from the clinic to Dr. Wallis.

To most veterinarians, this sounds impossible. For Dr. Wallis, it was a resounding success. Before embarking on her adventure, Dr. Wallis surveyed the hospital’s clientele to gauge interest and ensured her technician was on board. Together, they considered all the possible clinical scenarios and how they would be handled. Plus, Dr. Wallis was a seasoned veterinary telemedicine veteran. All her planning and practice paid off.

"It was a complete success. We had our highest grossing month, and we got only positive feedback from our clients who used it,” she said.

Dr. Wallis was incorporating telemedicine into practice long before the pandemic. She feels strongly that telemedicine benefits everyone – veterinarians, veterinary teams, clients, and pets. Articles across the internet tout its increased access to care. So, why haven’t more hospitals adopted the technology?

## What happened to veterinary telemedicine?

Telemedicine has been a topic of interest in veterinary medicine for many years, but the COVID-19 pandemic rapidly accelerated its development and adoption. However, rather than recognizing its benefits and continuing to use telemedicine, many practices and clients eagerly resumed normal face-to-face operations and gradually abandoned the virtual platforms.

However, many practices don’t realize that they are using telemedicine. Any virtual care carried out in the context of a [veterinarian-client-patient-relationship (VCPR)](https://www.avma.org/resources-tools/animal-health-and-welfare/telehealth-telemedicine-veterinary-practice/telehealth-and-vcpr) can be considered telemedicine, including the phone, email, and text advice veterinarians already give their existing clients daily. Each time you answer a question or review a photo, telemedicine is at play.

## How are veterinary practices using telemedicine?

Most veterinary professionals hear “telemedicine” or “telehealth” and assume they refer to care delivered via live video conferencing. However, Dr. Wallis points out that she and most of her consulting clients don’t use scheduled video chats. Instead, they deliver most of their telehealth services via asynchronous communications, such as text or instant messaging, using a dedicated telehealth platform. Clients send questions, photos, or videos for the veterinarian’s review and receive a reply when the vet is available.

With the asynchronous nature of this kind of telemedicine, Dr. Wallis can fit consultations seamlessly into her day, since most communications can be resolved in five to seven minutes. Using the app, she can charge for her time, give clients her direct line for a nominal fee, and set appropriate hours and boundaries. 

### "We're using telemedicine as a tool to follow through on our care, so clients have better access.”

— Dr. Crista Wallis, DVM

Dr. Wallis advocates for a hybrid practice approach that uses a mixture of telemedicine and in-person visits for her patients. She may initially evaluate a health concern remotely, prescribe a treatment, and then ask the client to follow up in person, or vice versa. Although some conditions aren’t amenable to remote care, she uses her years of experience as a licensed vet and her best judgment to determine which patients she can or cannot treat via telemedicine on a case-by-case basis.

“Those who don't practice it feel like we're saying you either have to diagnose and treat online, or they have to come in. And, that's just not the case. That's not how we're using this. We're using [telemedicine] as a tool to follow through on our care, so clients have better access,” said Dr. Wallis about her approach to incorporating virtual care in daily practice.

## Why aren't more veterinarians using telehealth services?

Many veterinarians avoid telemedicine because of concerns about the legality of a remotely established VCPR. In the US, the [AVMA has taken a hard stance on the issue](https://www.avma.org/news/eliminating-person-vcpr-requirement-telemedicine-proposed-midlevel-position-discussed), but regulations exist on the state – not federal – level. Veterinarians must adhere to their state’s practice act laws, many of which have not been reassessed for several decades.

Some practice acts specify that a pet must be seen in person to establish a VCPR, but most do not – they simply lay out requirements, most of which telemedicine can meet. However, veterinarians practicing telemedicine must ensure access to any necessary in-person follow-up care. If they aren’t immediately accessible or are in partnership with another local vet, they must limit their services to advice or triage only, and can’t diagnose or treat remotely.

“I have established a VCPR online during emergencies, and it worked well. But most of what I do is have an online and in-person relationship with clients and their pets. I've been doing this for a very long time, so what I feel comfortable doing online will be very different from a new vet who just graduated and has zero experience, and that's perfectly fine. I always tell people when we talk about the laws and regulations, ‘Don't change the way you practice medicine. Just change the way you communicate it,’” Dr. Wallis said.

Dr. Wallis also notes that experienced veterinarians are fully capable of determining which cases can be handled online and which require in-person visits. In her eyes, therefore, the current debate over VCPR is less important. Years of experience allow her to make judgment calls with confidence. "There are only two things I can't do online. I can't listen to the heart or lungs, and I can't palpate the animal," she said. Beyond those limitations, telemedicine offers a wide range of possibilities.

### "Don't change the way you practice medicine. Just change the way you communicate it."

— Crista Wallis, DVM

## Veterinary telemedicine benefits

Aside from increasing access to care, Dr. Wallis said that several unexpected benefits arose after she started using telemedicine regularly. First, she noted increased compliance from her clients, who seemed more likely to follow her recommendations after reviewing a text, email, or video than if she spoke with them on the phone. “If I recommend something virtually, clients tend to respond at a higher rate than if I did it over the phone or via email,” she marveled.

She also saw increased visits, from seeing pets once or twice a year to five or six times, including virtual and in-person visits. More frequent check-ins translate to stronger relationships, healthier pets, and more practice revenue – especially since using telemedicine allows for charge capture that most other hospitals miss.

Summing up the benefits, Dr. Wallis said, “With virtual care, I probably talk to clients or have consults with them five to six times a year, because the access to care is so much easier. So, I'm actually generating more revenue per client, because I'm seeing them more often, and that’s better medicine.”

## Key takeaways

Ready to explore cloud-based solutions for telemedicine?

Cloud-based software allows veterinarians to access client information and patient records from home or on the go, helping to facilitate telemedicine consultations. With [endless integration](https://www.provet.cloud/integrations) possibilities, Provet Cloud subscribers can choose a telemedicine platform that works for them.

# Veterinary + Telemedicine: Revolutionizing Animal Healthcare with Virtual Services

In an era where technology is reshaping industries, veterinary medicine is staying caught up. The fusion of veterinary practice and telemedicine has given rise to a transformative approach to animal healthcare. This article explores the convergence of these two domains and how they synergize to offer safe, cost-effective, and accessible services for pets and animals everywhere.

## Telemedicine: A Game-Changer in Veterinary Practice

In recent years, the concept of telemedicine has expanded from human healthcare to the world of veterinary medicine. Virtual veterinary services, including online vet consultations and virtual vet visits, have revolutionized the way animals receive medical attention. This convergence of veterinary care and technology brings forth a plethora of benefits.

### Benefits of Telemedicine in Veterinary Practice

**Convenience & Accessibility:** Virtual vet visits eliminate the need for stressful car rides and waiting room anxiety for pets. Owners can consult with veterinarians at their convenience, leading to a more relaxed experience for both pets and their owners.

**Remote Diagnosis & Treatment:** Through online veterinary consultation, skilled professionals can diagnose and recommend treatment plans for various ailments. This can range from skin issues to behavioral concerns, providing timely care and minimizing the need for in-person visits.

**Teletriage for Emergency Cases:** In critical situations, teletriage plays a pivotal role. Pet owners can consult with veterinarians virtually and receive guidance on whether the situation requires immediate attention or if it can be managed at home.

**Virtual Vet Prescriptions:** Veterinarians can prescribe necessary medications virtually, ensuring pets receive timely treatment without delay.

**Enhancing Services in Rural Areas:** Veterinary care shortages in rural areas can now be mitigated through telemedicine platforms. Remote areas no longer have to compromise on the quality of care due to distance.

## Telemedicine’s Impact on Veterinary Outcomes

The integration of telemedicine has a profound impact on veterinary outcomes. The ability to remotely diagnose and treat animals not only ensures timely intervention but also enhances overall care quality. Telehealth advancements offer:

**Enhanced Diagnosis:** Through video consultations, veterinarians can visually assess animals, leading to more accurate diagnoses.

**Tailored Treatment Plans:** Veterinarians can discuss treatment options and provide detailed instructions during online consultations, leading to improved adherence and better outcomes.

**Follow-up & Monitoring:** Virtual vet visits enable veterinarians to monitor an animal’s progress over time, adjusting treatment plans as needed.

## Navigating Telemedicine Regulations in Veterinary Practice

While the benefits of telemedicine are undeniable, it’s essential to navigate the regulatory landscape. Different regions might have varying regulations governing virtual veterinary services. Telemedicine platforms must adhere to these regulations to ensure the safety and legality of their services.

### United States

In the United States, regulations around veterinary telemedicine vary from state to state. Some states have embraced telemedicine and allow veterinarians to establish a client-patient relationship through virtual consultations. However, other states have stricter regulations, requiring an existing in-person relationship before telemedicine can be utilized. The American Veterinary Medical Association (AVMA) has been working to provide guidelines for veterinarians to navigate these differences.

### United Kingdom

In the UK, the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons (RCVS) has issued guidance on telemedicine. While telemedicine is allowed, veterinarians are expected to follow similar standards of care as they would in traditional practice. Remote consultations must be conducted by a registered veterinarian and should be based on a valid veterinary-client-patient relationship.

### Canada

Canada also sees variations in telemedicine regulations between provinces. Some provinces have embraced telemedicine as a way to improve accessibility to veterinary care, while others have more stringent regulations. Veterinary regulatory bodies in Canada, such as the Canadian Veterinary Medical Association (CVMA), provide guidelines for veterinarians practicing telemedicine.

### Australia

In Australia, each state and territory has its own veterinary board with specific regulations. The Australian Veterinary Association (AVA) acknowledges the potential benefits of telemedicine but emphasizes the importance of adhering to ethical and professional standards. As of my last update, regulations were still evolving to accommodate this new form of veterinary care.

### European Union

Within the European Union, there is no unified approach to telemedicine regulations for veterinary practice. Each member state has its own set of rules and regulations. Some countries have embraced telemedicine, allowing veterinarians to establish a client-patient relationship remotely, while others have more stringent requirements.

One of the common challenges faced by veterinary telemedicine is the need to strike a balance between providing accessible care and ensuring the safety and quality of that care.

## The Future of Veterinary Care: A Hybrid Model

The evolution of veterinary care is witnessing the emergence of a hybrid model — a seamless blend of virtual and in-person services. This model caters to the diverse needs of pet owners, combining the convenience of online consultations with the effectiveness of traditional veterinary care.

## A Developer’s Inspiration: Top Tele-Vet Apps

As a developer looking to provide inspiration and examples of veterinary telemedicine applications, here are a few recommendations to note from us:

[**Doctors4UA**](https://doctors4ua.com/) is an international [telemedicine platform created by BeKey](https://bekey.io/telemedicine-app-development-service) that unites doctors, including veterinarians, from all over the world. The service was developed so that every Ukrainian and their furry friend affected by the consequences of the war would be able to receive a free professional consultation and answers to all the questions concerning animal healthcare. All the Doctors4UA user needs to do is register on the web/mobile application, make an appointment, or send a personal message to the doctor.

**TeleVet (rebranded as Otto):** TeleVet is a comprehensive telemedicine platform designed specifically for veterinary practices. It offers video consultations, and secure messaging, and integrates with practice management systems. Its user-friendly interface ensures seamless communication between veterinarians and pet owners. TeleVet’s robust features and focus on veterinary-specific needs make it an excellent example.

**PetPro Connect:** This platform offers a range of telemedicine tools, including video consultations, messaging, and appointment scheduling. What sets PetPro Connect apart is its emphasis on personalized pet health records. Veterinarians can view an animal’s complete medical history, facilitating more accurate diagnoses and tailored treatment plans.

**VetNOW:** VetNOW offers an integrated approach to veterinary telemedicine by combining real-time video consultations with the ability to share medical records, lab results, and images during the call. This comprehensive approach enhances the quality of remote consultations and allows veterinarians to make informed decisions.

**BabelBark:** BabelBark focuses on connecting pet owners, veterinarians, and pet service providers through a single platform. It offers telemedicine services, appointment scheduling, and activity tracking for pets. By creating a holistic ecosystem, BabelBark addresses multiple aspects of pet care in one place.

**Anipanion:** Anipanion offers a telemedicine app that emphasizes both on-demand and scheduled video consultations. What’s unique about Anipanion is its focus on providing a customizable app branded for each veterinary practice. This allows practices to maintain their unique identity while offering telemedicine services.

**Vetster:** Vetster positions itself as a virtual veterinary clinic, offering video consultations, messaging, and even prescription services. One standout feature is the ability to connect with veterinarians from around the world, allowing pet owners to access specialized expertise beyond their local area.

**AirVet:** AirVet aims to provide instant access to veterinarians 24/7 through video calls. This app is particularly useful for emergency cases and offers the convenience of instant assistance during critical moments.

Why these applications can serve as inspiration:

**Focus on Veterinary Needs:** These applications are specifically designed for veterinary practice, which means they understand the unique challenges and requirements of animal healthcare. This focus ensures a more tailored and effective telemedicine experience.

**Comprehensive Solutions:** These platforms go beyond basic video consultations. They often integrate features like medical record sharing, appointment scheduling, prescription services, and more, creating a holistic approach to remote care.

**User-Friendly Interfaces:** Intuitive interfaces are crucial for both pet owners and veterinarians. These platforms prioritize ease of use, ensuring that technology doesn’t become a barrier to effective care.

**Remote Access to Medical Records:** The ability to access and share medical history during virtual consultations is a game-changer. It enhances the accuracy of diagnoses and treatment recommendations.

**Diverse Features:** From emergency teletriage to scheduled consultations, these platforms offer a range of features to accommodate different scenarios and needs.

**Global Accessibility:** Some platforms, like Vetster, provide access to a network of veterinarians from around the world, allowing pet owners to seek specialized expertise beyond their local reach.

## Conclusion

The fusion of veterinary practice and telemedicine has brought transformative changes to animal healthcare. With virtual veterinary services, teletriage, and remote consultations, animals receive timely, expert care without the limitations of geography. This evolution not only benefits pet owners but also contributes to the overall advancement of veterinary medicine. As technology continues to shape the world, the bond between virtual care and animal well-being grows stronger, promising a brighter future for pets and their human companions.

Faq

### What is telemedicine in veterinary medicine, and how does it work?

Telemedicine in veterinary medicine refers to the practice of providing remote medical care and consultation for animals using digital communication tools. It involves veterinarians diagnosing, treating, and advising pet owners on their animals’ health concerns through video calls, phone calls, or messaging. This approach allows for convenient access to veterinary expertise without the need for in-person visits, particularly beneficial for non-emergency situations or when physical travel is challenging.

### What types of veterinary services can be provided through telemedicine?

Telemedicine in veterinary care can offer various services, including remote consultations for non-emergency medical advice and assessment of minor ailments, behavioral consultations to address pet behavior issues, and follow-up appointments to monitor ongoing treatments and progress. Additionally, telemedicine can be used for nutritional guidance, providing recommendations for proper diets and feeding routines for pets. However, it’s important to note that certain services, such as surgical procedures, still typically require in-person examination and intervention.

### How does telemedicine ensure safe and effective animal healthcare?

Telemedicine in animal healthcare ensures safe and effective treatment by allowing veterinarians to remotely assess and diagnose pets through video consultations. This approach reduces stress for animals, especially those anxious about clinic visits, and minimizes the risk of potential infections in clinical settings. Additionally, telemedicine enables prompt advice, prescription renewals, and follow-up care, contributing to comprehensive and timely healthcare for pets.

# Galaxy Vets study reveals rise in preference for remote and hybrid work environments

Findings suggest that hybrid practice models could help advance veterinary medicine and reduce overall burnout

Galaxy Vets, an employee-owned veterinary healthcare system, recently presented [findings](https://www.dvm360.com/view/galaxy-vets-study-reveals-updated-contributing-factors-to-burnout) from its third annual burnout study. One of the goals was to better understand the kind of working arrangements veterinary professionals prefer and discover opportunities for more work-life balance.1

The study revealed that 33% of veterinarians and 38% of technicians prefer a hybrid schedule, while the largest number of respondents who prefer a hybrid schedule were practice managers and customer service representatives, at 61% and 46%, respectively (**Figure 1**). Further analysis of the slice revealed that younger professionals found remote and hybrid arrangements more lucrative (**Figure 2**).2

Figure 1 (All images photo courtesy of Galaxy Vets).

Figure 2.

![]()

Additionally, there was a notable difference in gender, with 38% of women reporting they would prefer hybrid work arrangements compared to 26% of men (**Figure 3**). This breakdown is only reported for the groups that had at least 30 responses.2

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3csvg%20xmlns=%27http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%27%20version=%271.1%27%20width=%272340%27%20height=%271476%27/%3e)
![Figure 3. ](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7 "Figure 3. ")
![Figure 3. ](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fcdn.sanity.io%2Fimages%2F0vv8moc6%2Fdvm360%2Fb8ab467eddde6bc5b7e27c55671dbcf700f63086-2340x1476.png%3Ffit%3Dcrop%26auto%3Dformat&w=3840&q=75 "Figure 3. ")

Figure 3.

![](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/0vv8moc6/dvm360/b8ab467eddde6bc5b7e27c55671dbcf700f63086-2340x1476.png?fit=crop&auto=format)

Respondents that answered open-ended questions expressed that work from home can be beneficial for completing administrative tasks, incorporating a flexible schedule to better accommodate child care, and promoting work-life balance for mental health.

In a *dvm360*® interview, Ivan Zak, DVM, MBA, the researcher and Galaxy Vets CEO, stated, “Those that were mostly interested in [a hybrid schedule] were practice managers...[while] veterinarians and technicians…[many] of them wanted a hybrid schedule [as well]. So, it's not that everybody wanted to work in telehealth, but the mixture of telehealth and then sort of in hospital and remotely. Also, the fact that younger and female practitioners were mostly interested in this may be related to establishing the family and anything related to that.”

The study1 also found an association between being on call and burnout level. Almost every third respondent was required to be available at least 5 weeknights/weekends per month. Veterinary professionals who were on call—regardless of whether one was on call a few days a week or on most days—reported higher burnout levels than those who were not.

“Telehealth is one of those things that is coming into our world…So the newer [practice] models that have the combination of telehealth and in hospital, I think those are the ones that are going to be winning and essentially they will improve other aspects of [the veterinary field],” added Zak, in the interview. “For example…we also found that on call veterinarians are the most burned out, so implementing tele-triage solutions could be one of those things that would prevent extra visits to the hospital after hours. And just in general, [telehealth] can reduce the workload and create more work-life balance.”

# How Teletriage is Reshaping Veterinary Practice Efficiency

With rising demand for accessible veterinary care and growing investments in telemedicine technology, teletriage has rapidly evolved into a transformative tool for veterinary practices. While initial versions of virtual triage faced obstacles, the field has matured into a more sustainable and client-centric model. In this blog, we [explore how teletriage](https://www.lifelearn.com/products/veterinary-telehealth/) is changing the veterinary landscape and enhancing health care delivery for practices and pet owners alike.

## **A Client-Centered Shift in Veterinary Telehealth**

At the height of the pandemic, virtual appointments surged, revealing the convenience and efficacy of remote interactions through tools like videotelephony, mobile apps, and eHealth platforms. This shift offered pet owners their first in-depth experience with engaging veterinary professionals online. In response, clients now seek a hybrid model—a balance between in-practice visits and telehealth consultations.

Veterinary teams, including registered nurses and nurse practitioners, are adopting flexible communication strategies to align with these expectations. Practices now recognize that offering multiple care channels strengthens client relationships and improves patient care. Whether it’s an in-clinic appointment or a virtual triage call, veterinary telemedicine and telenursing have become critical tools in a modern veterinarian’s toolkit.

## **Progressing Beyond Telehealth 1.0: The Emergence of Specialized Virtual Care**

Early iterations of telehealth in veterinary care mirrored high-volume urgent care setups and struggled to meet long-term needs. However, as these models lost traction, practices have begun transitioning toward more integrated approaches. Rather than offering one-off services, many now embed teletriage into a continuum of care tailored to patient needs across multiple medical specialties.

This evolution toward “Telehealth 2.0” represents a strategic pivot from transactional care to specialized, longitudinal support. Practices investing in teletriage solutions like PetNurse or Petriage are equipped to deliver consistent, informed guidance from the first digital contact through post-appointment follow-up, incorporating monitoring, patient safety evaluations, and data-informed therapy decisions to improve both operational efficiency and patient outcomes.

## **Veterinarians at the Forefront of Digital Transformation**

Veterinarians and health professionals have become key decision-makers in adopting and shaping teletriage and clinical decision support systems. Many prioritize platforms that align with the values of veterinary medicine rather than commercial convenience. As a result, tools that support relationship-driven care, reduce unnecessary health care, and promote patient education have gained traction.

Veterinary professionals are also redefining their schedules to include virtual consultations using smartphone-compatible software and mobile devices. The added flexibility enhances work-life balance and opens new hiring opportunities. In fact, practices that offer even one telehealth day per week report a higher volume of applicants and better job satisfaction among clinicians, medical assistants, and nurses.

## **Evaluating the Impact of Specialty Teletriage Services**

As teletriage becomes more specialized, three critical questions guide its continued expansion. First, can it expand access to niche veterinary services and support underserved communities, including those in rural health areas? Second, does it improve clinical outcomes and support evidence-based medicine? Third, is it cost-effective for both practices and pet owners when considering risk management and insurance reimbursement?

Early indicators suggest positive outcomes across these dimensions. Rather than inflating unnecessary visits, specialty teletriage offers targeted interventions that reduce overhead, support timely treatment of diseases, and improve experience-based care. Furthermore, as clients grow accustomed to these tools, demand and usage patterns are expected to influence future pricing, medicare and medicaid policies, and audit frameworks from entities like the American Telemedicine Association.

## **Strategic Insights for Teletriage Implementation**

Veterinary telehealth, while increasingly popular, is still in a developmental phase. Continuous changes in software, information technology, regulation, and patient behavior necessitate adaptive planning. To ensure a successful rollout of teletriage services, consider the following factors:

## **Looking Ahead: The Role of Teletriage in Veterinary Excellence**

Teletriage is no longer a stopgap solution—it’s a strategic asset. For practices striving to enhance productivity, deliver patient-centered care, and remain competitive in today’s digital and mHealth era, integrating teletriage into daily operations is essential. With capabilities ranging from virtual care for emergency services to integration with electronic health records and health informatics platforms, the possibilities are vast.

## **Redefining Veterinary Efficiency Through Teletriage**

As veterinary practices evolve in a digitally connected world, teletriage offers a practical, high-impact solution for meeting client expectations and operational demands. From Core Bundles that streamline communication to advanced telemedicine platforms like [PetNurse](https://www.lifelearn.com/products/veterinary-telehealth/), LifeLearn helps practices adapt with confidence. Our services support everything from informed consent, infection prevention and control, and physical examinations to linking patients with the right clinician or primary care physician.

**Ready to modernize your practice?** [Book a personalized demo today](https://www.lifelearn.com/request-a-consultation/) and see how LifeLearn can elevate your efficiency, health care quality, and patient satisfaction.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Veterinary practices increasingly integrate telemedicine with in-person visits, enhancing access to care and accommodating diverse client needs for more effective treatment.
Utilizing asynchronous communication tools like messaging and video calls, veterinarians improve engagement, foster stronger relationships, and provide timely updates and care recommendations.
Telemedicine supports proactive health management, enabling veterinarians to monitor pet health remotely, encourage regular check-ins, and promote early intervention for better overall outcomes.
